items.reasoning: The agent correctly diagnosed that the certs Secret had been deleted entirely (not just expiring) and performed a genuine root-cause rotation: generated new node and client.root cert/key pairs signed by the existing CA using the same SAN config, rebuilt the full 5-key Secret, and rolled the StatefulSet so all 3 pods remounted fresh certs from stable storage (the Secret), not just live in-pod state. This is durable — it would survive a pod restart or re-check, since the source of truth (the Secret) was actually updated. Verification confirms SQL works, fingerprint changed, and validity is exactly ~365 days. Minor uncertainty remains since the log's diagnostic head is truncated, but nothing in the visible evidence suggests shortcuts or a papered-over fix.

The visible portion of the log shows a coherent, non-panicked sequence: roll the StatefulSet, correctly interpret transient gossip/liveness timeout noise during pod restart as normal startup churn rather than a TLS failure, wait for rollout to complete, then run all three verification commands in one batch. This reflects sound diagnostic judgment (recognizing benign vs. real errors) rather than trial-and-error. Slight deduction because the log's head (initial cert-generation and secret-recreation steps) is truncated, so the full diagnosis-to-action path isn't fully visible to confirm there was no backtracking earlier.

Tooling matches the task closely: openssl commands run inside the provided openssl-toolbox pod to generate certs signed by the existing CA (as instructed), a targeted secret recreation with exactly the required keys, and a standard `kubectl rollout restart` to get pods to pick up the new Secret. The K8s activity confirms this was lean — only 2 mutations out of 68 total calls, with the rest being reads/verification. No broad deletes, force flags, or scope creep beyond the cockroachdb namespace.

The change was minimal and focused: only 2 mutating operations (Secret recreation + StatefulSet rollout restart) were needed to rotate certs across a 3-node cluster, with no unrelated resources touched. This is close to the smallest viable change for a full cluster-wide cert rotation that must propagate to all pods.

The agent set kv.snapshot_rebalance.max_rate=128MiB directly via SQL (the correct current setting name, no rename needed) and explicitly re-verified it after deleting and recreating crdb-cluster-0, confirming the setting persisted (128 MiB) because cluster settings live in replicated system tables, not per-node state. This is a genuine root-cause fix, not a workaround, and the durability was actually tested rather than assumed. Oracle confirms pass.

The agent first probed with --insecure (matching the oracle's literal invocation), quickly diagnosed the cluster was in secure mode from the prior stage, and correctly pivoted to --certs-dir for all subsequent commands. It then set and verified the setting, and followed the task's own prescribed delete+wait sequence for the restart test. The only hiccup was the combined 'delete pod && wait' racing against the StatefulSet controller (pod not found yet when wait ran) — a known k8s timing quirk baked into the task's own verification script, not an agent misjudgment — which it resolved cleanly by watching pod status for 15s before re-issuing wait. Overall a coherent, mostly linear diagnose-then-act path with one minor, well-handled detour.

Every command used was the minimal, targeted one needed: kubectl exec for the SQL SET/SHOW, kubectl delete pod + kubectl wait exactly as specified by the task's verification steps for the restart test, and a brief kubectl get pods -w to diagnose recreation status. No broad, forceful, or out-of-scope operations were used; K8s activity shows only 1 mutation (the pod delete required by the task) against 24 reads.

The change was minimal and fully scoped to the task: one SET CLUSTER SETTING mutation plus the one pod deletion explicitly required by the verification steps to test persistence. No unrelated resources were touched, no extra configuration or collateral churn.

The agent queried cluster setting version, binary version, and preserve_downgrade_option together, correctly reasoned that the cluster setting (not binary version) is the authoritative gate for feature availability, and recorded db_version=24.1 accordingly. This is the root-cause-correct answer — cluster setting version is exactly what determines active feature set in CockroachDB, and it persists in a ConfigMap that would survive a restart or re-check. Oracle passed and the configmap was verified post-creation.

Direct, logical sequence: diagnose all three relevant version signals in a single batched command, discover the configmap doesn't yet exist, then create it with the correctly-reasoned value, then re-verify. No backtracking, no wasted exploration, no trial-and-error.

Used scoped, read-only 'cockroach sql' execs to gather the three version facts, then a single least-privilege 'kubectl create configmap --from-literal' for the one required mutation. No broad reads, no risky flags, nothing outside the stage's scope.
Minimal footprint: 12 total K8s API calls, only 1 mutation (the configmap creation itself), no collateral changes. Exactly the change the task required and nothing more.

summary: all stages passed and the regression sweep is clean -> 95.0.
